title: What Consumer Discretionary Sells: The Want, Not the Need
quick_definition: Consumer Discretionary is the sector of the optional purchase — the goods and services a household buys with the money left after necessities: retail and e-commerce, apparel and home durables, restaurants and hotels, and cars. One label, four very different businesses, united by a customer who can always say "not this year."
explainer_covers: The everyday tour: this is the sector the learner already shops in — the sneaker (Nike), the latte (Starbucks), the cart (Amazon), the room (Marriott), the car (Tesla) — which makes it the easiest sector to recognize and one of the trickiest to analyze, because familiarity masquerades as insight.

The four industry groups, walked as four business models: Distribution & Retail (move other people's goods — thin margins, huge volumes, Z2.1) · Consumer Durables & Apparel (make and brand your own goods — the margin lives in the label, Z2.2) · Consumer Services (sell an experience, often through franchisees — restaurants, hotels, leisure, Z2.3) · Automobiles & Components (the sector's heavy industry, Z2.4). Four models, one wallet.

The GICS boundary — read this before you mis-shelve a company. Amazon sits here while its profit engine is platform economics taught in sector-IT; Tesla sits here while trading like a tech stock; Google/Meta/Netflix are in Communication Services; and the sector's twin, Consumer Staples (the non-deferrable purchase — toothpaste, groceries), is a separate, not-yet-built module. The label is administrative; this module teaches the economics, which travel.
What binds the four: the discretionary customer — the demand behavior Z1.2 defines, the cycle exposure Z5.1 traces, and the war for preference Z3.3/Z3.4 fight.

The door onward: this is the sector framework Equity Research's Z2.3 points to — its retailer worked contrast ("same-store sales, traffic, and margin") is taught in full in Zone 3. Someone who wants to analyze a consumer company starts from exactly this understanding of what the sector sells.

title: The Economics of the Deferrable Purchase: Demand You Have to Win
quick_definition: Discretionary demand is demand the customer can defer, downgrade, or skip — the opposite of a staple. That single fact makes the sector cyclical (the wallet shrinks first here), competitive (every want has a near-substitute), and brand-obsessed (preference must be manufactured, because necessity won't do the selling for you).
explainer_covers: Wants vs. needs, made precise. A staple gets bought in a recession; a want waits. Discretionary purchases are funded by the residual wallet — income after necessities — so the sector's demand is a leveraged claim on household confidence: when incomes wobble, discretionary spending falls by more (the amplification Z5.1 walks through the cycle). ★G314 is that deferral option.

The deferral option is the customer's power. "Not this year" disciplines every business in the sector — and the bigger the ticket (cars, furniture, vacations), the stronger the option, which is why durables and autos are the sector's highest-beta corners.

The third pricing regime (the wave-one trilogy completes). Energy's producer cannot set price (a barrel is a barrel — `sector-energy.z1.2`); software sets price on zero marginal cost and lock-in (★G293); the consumer company sits between: it earns pricing power only if it wins the customer's head. Nike charges what Nike charges because it's Nike — the pricing power is real, manufactured, and perishable. This is why brand (Z3.4) is this sector's core productive asset.

Where the wallet flows when it's squeezed — the teaser. Discretionary customers don't cut evenly: they rocket in categories they love and trade down everywhere else — the polarization Z3.3 teaches as the sector's demand-structure law.

A word with two lives. "Discretionary" here means the customer can defer the purchase — not the fund world's "discretionary" (human-judgment) investing (★G131) or the advisor's discretionary mandate. Same word, unrelated axis.

The staples seam (stated, not built). The mirror sector — non-deferrable demand, defensive in downturns — is a wave-two module; when it's built, it back-links this global for the boundary rather than re-homing it.
